summary: McKenzie recounts a day where his planned prototype work was derailed by helping college friends migrate an old VBulletin forum to his VPS. He describes the horror of the forum's previous security state, including outdated PHP, MySQL running as root, and a trivially guessable password. He pivots to praising VPS hosting over shared hosting for technically skilled developers, noting he runs multiple sites cheaply on Slicehost. The post closes with his hope that his new project will need serious infrastructure scaling.
key_points: Technical favors for friends can consume an entire day of productive development time
Many small web applications run with shockingly poor security configurations that invite exploitation
VPS hosting offers dramatic advantages over shared hosting for developers with Linux administration skills
McKenzie runs multiple businesses and blogs on cheap VPS instances for about $20/month each
money_quotes: Nice update interface, very user friendly — a pity about it taking 400 clickthroughs to complete installation (I kept track).
I wanted to cry when I saw its previous state — using an outdated PHP and MySql running as root, naturally, with a four letter password the same as the name of the previous site owner.
I continue to be amazed by how awesome it is to have a VPS over shared hosting, provided that you have some skill in Linux administration.
First Prayer of Computer Programmers with Business Sense: Please Lord, send me scaleability issues.
Suffice it to say I got a lot of use out of rand() today.
key_insight: The best problem a software entrepreneur can have is needing to scale, and cheap VPS hosting makes it easy to run multiple small businesses affordably while maintaining control.
